Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional because I couldn't always make it to classes with my schedule. I'd block out chunks of time, usually around 23 hours a week, where I'd dive deep into the material. I'm very hands-on, so I'd often be working through practice problems, creating diagrams, or physically writing things out rather than just reading. I found that working in longer sessions when I could focus worked better for me than scattered short bursts throughout the day.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, studying at the same times, but then life would throw something at me and I'd have to cram everything into a few intense days. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied most intensely. I tried to be regular, but honestly, consistency was one of my biggest struggles even though I always got the work done.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment details or download materials for studying offline. Sometimes I'd log in to see if there were any announcements or deadline changes I might have missed since my attendance was so low. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often than I would have otherwise because there was something almost satisfying about completing those challenges. But I wasn't someone who just browsed around the LMS casually.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me—it was always more of a tool I used when necessary. The gamified elements helped a bit because they gave me little goals to hit, which appealed to my need for tangible progress. But I'd say my routine was more about what I did with the materials after downloading them than spending time on the platform itself. I just couldn't get into the habit of being constantly logged in.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could watch them at my own pace and rewind parts I didn't understand. I watched over two hours worth of video content because I could engage with it physically—pausing to take notes, rewinding to practice problems alongside the instructor. The discussion posts were helpful too when I actually participated because hearing other perspectives helped things click. Anything I could interact with actively rather than passively read was valuable to me.
Q6: I often skipped or minimally used the reading materials that were just dense PDFs with no interactive elements. They didn't match how I learn—I need to do something with information, not just absorb it through reading. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant if I'd already grasped the concept through videos or practice. I also ignored a lot of the optional discussion threads because my anxiety made it hard to put myself out there when it wasn't required.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. I needed to feel like I was building toward something concrete, not just checking boxes. The gamified tasks were surprisingly motivating because they gave me immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from what I'd be assessed on, I struggled to justify spending time on it when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, even when I knew they might help. Required activities had this weight to them that pushed through my anxiety and got me moving. With optional stuff, my brain would spiral into questions like 'is this really the best use of my time?' and I'd often talk myself out of it. The gamified optional tasks were different though—they felt more like rewards than obligations, which made them easier to approach.
Q9: Absolutely, this was huge for me. I'd download lecture slides, videos when possible, and any practice materials, then work through them on paper or with physical flashcards. I had notebooks full of hand-drawn diagrams and worked problems that would never show up in any system. I also used external YouTube videos and textbooks from the library to supplement what we covered. My real learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: Most of my actual studying wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours working through problems by hand, teaching concepts to myself out loud, or using physical models to understand spatial relationships. When I was preparing for exams, I'd be at my desk with printed materials and notebooks, not logged into anything. My peer interactions often happened in person or over text, not through the platform's discussion features.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download or take detailed notes from it, and then never open it again on the LMS. My assignment scores show that this worked for me—I was learning effectively, just not in a way that left digital footprints. I'd sometimes reference the same downloaded PDF for weeks while the system would only show that single initial access.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and everything felt overwhelming, even though my stress was generally manageable. When multiple deadlines converged or when I felt behind because of missed classes, engaging felt almost impossible. My low attendance meant I sometimes felt disconnected from the course community, which made it harder to reach out for help. Those periods required me to really dig deep and push through despite feeling like I was drowning.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. When life got complicated, the LMS was the first thing I'd cut because I knew I could learn from downloaded materials. Fatigue definitely played a role too—after a long day, logging into yet another platform felt exhausting. Confusion sometimes kept me away because I didn't want to face material I didn't understand, which I know wasn't the healthiest response but anxiety does that.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. My anxiety would sometimes hijack my attention even when I was technically 'studying.' I'd scroll through materials without really processing them, just going through the motions. Those sessions felt wasted, and I'd often have to redo them later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts because putting my thoughts out there for others to see triggered my anxiety badly. Even when I knew participating would help my understanding, I'd find reasons to delay. Sometimes I'd avoid starting assignments until closer to the deadline because the pressure actually helped me focus through the anxiety. It's not a healthy pattern, but the avoidance was real even when I knew better.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'd be logged in, checking requirements, downloading materials, submitting work. Without that external pressure, I'd drift toward offline studying and forget to engage with the platform. The gamified tasks with their own mini-deadlines helped create more regular touchpoints, which I actually appreciated.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me, probably more than it should have. Positive feedback would boost my confidence and make me want to engage more. But if feedback was critical, even constructively so, I'd need time to process it before I could face the course again. My anxiety meant I'd sometimes avoid checking grades or comments because I was afraid of what I'd see, even when I'd done well.
Q18: The course structure with its flexibility actually helped me manage despite my low attendance. Having materials available online meant I could catch up on my own terms. The gamified elements added structure that I wouldn't have created for myself, giving me smaller goals to work toward. However, the structure also assumed more regular engagement than I could maintain, which sometimes made me feel like I was always playing catch-up.
Q19: The availability of downloadable materials made engagement possible for someone like me who learns best offline. The gamified tasks made engagement feel less intimidating and more achievable. What made it harder was when activities required synchronous participation or assumed I'd been in class. Discussion requirements were tough because my anxiety made public participation feel high-stakes even in a digital space.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things repeatedly without actually making progress—I've done that myself. They might be searching for clarity or reassurance but not finding it, so they keep coming back. Or they could be struggling to understand the material and spending lots of time trying to figure things out before they feel ready to submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My attendance was really low and my LMS engagement wasn't high, but I still earned a distinction. I did the real work offline—downloading materials once, studying intensively from physical notes, and putting serious effort into my assignments. The platform only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the course content. Performance and visible activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, submission timing, and maybe consistency of engagement. They're probably good at flagging students who've completely disappeared. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone actually understood what they accessed, how much offline work they did, or what their emotional state was while studying. They can't capture learning that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, not just passively receiving it. It's when I'm asking questions, making connections, and feeling invested in understanding rather than just completing. For me, engagement often looks like physical activity—writing, drawing, practicing—rather than screen time. It's a mental and emotional investment in actually learning, not just going through motions to get grades.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much anxiety I pushed through to participate, or the hours I spent with physical notes and practice problems. It wouldn't capture the peer study sessions that happened over coffee instead of through the platform, or the times I taught myself concepts through trial and error with hands-on practice. My resilience in climbing from a difficult starting point to a distinction—that story isn't in any log file.
